nativeRange.habitat: The Lemon Tetra inhabits minor tributaries, smaller rivers, oxbow lakes, and seasonally flooded forests, tending to avoid the main river channels. These environments typically feature sandy substrate with fine-leaved vegetation and accumulations of leaf litter.
nativeRange.biotope: Blackwater/clearwater Amazonian tributaries; Rio Tapajós basin and lower Rio Xingu system, Pará state.

diet.notes: Accepts a wide range of foods including quality flake and micro-pellet; benefits from regular offerings of live or frozen foods such as bloodworm, mosquito larvae, Daphnia, and Moina. Wild diet includes small invertebrates, crustaceans, filamentous algae, and fallen fruit.

avoidWith: predatory fish that can swallow small tetras

imageCandidates.notes: Cupr78up, 4208x2368. Sharp group of lemon tetras among driftwood roots in a biotope-style setup matching the species' care notes; vivid yellow bodies, red eyes, schooling behavior on display.
SOK, 2637x1435. Tack-sharp full lateral view of a single vibrant individual; best diagnostic detail of the yellow body, bright red eye, and black-edged yellow dorsal and anal fins.
Bloopityboop, 4032x3024. Group shot with a tack-sharp central fish clearly showing the yellow-and-black anal fin and dorsal accents; multiple individuals represent the schooling habit.

summary: The Lemon Tetra is a peaceful, long-lived schooling fish from Brazil's Tapajós basin, generally considered a good choice for beginners. Its pale yellow body and black-edged fins intensify in good water conditions. Best kept in groups of six or more.
careNotes: The Lemon Tetra is generally considered one of the more straightforward tetras for beginners. Its yellow body and vivid yellow-and-black fin accents intensify noticeably in well-maintained water and in larger groups. The species is widely captive-bred, making wild-caught specimens uncommon in the trade. Schools of 8–10 or more have been observed to show notably better coloration and more natural behavior than smaller groups. A planted setup with driftwood and some leaf litter tends to bring out the best color and reduce shyness. The Lemon Tetra has not been reported to nip fins. It tolerates a fairly wide range of water parameters, though softer and slightly acidic conditions closer to the Rio Tapajós origin may improve long-term health and breeding success.
breedingNotes: The Lemon Tetra is an egg-scatterer that provides no parental care, and adults will consume eggs if not removed from the breeding tank promptly. Breeding is considered moderately straightforward. A separate, dimly lit tank fitted with fine-leaved plants or a mesh substrate to catch and protect the eggs is recommended. Slightly acidic to neutral water with temperature toward the upper end of the species' range tends to encourage spawning. Fry hatch within 24–36 hours and are initially very small; first foods of Paramecium or infusoria are appropriate, progressing to Artemia nauplii and microworms as the fry grow. Males have been observed briefly defending loose spawning territories during the process.

additional.notes: Cross-checked size (3.8 cm SL max), temperature (23–28°C), pH (5.5–8.0), hardness (≤25 dGH), and native range (Tapajós basin). Size averaged with SeriouslyFish (35–40 mm SL) to give 3.5–4.5 cm. Aquarium Co-Op (primary source per source-urls.json) had no species-specific page for this species; SeriouslyFish promoted to primary.

additional.notes: Cross-checked size (~5 cm / 2 in), temperature (72–82°F = 22–28°C), pH (5.5–8.0), lifespan (6–8 yrs), tank size (20 gal = 76 L). Temperature and pH consistent with other sources. Size slightly higher at 5 cm; averaged down to 4.5 cm max. Lifespan source for min value of 5 yrs (combined with controller key data of 5–8 yrs).

fish.breedingNotes: The Lemon Tetra scatters eggs with no parental care; adults will consume eggs and must be removed after spawning. A separate breeding tank with a mesh bottom or fine-leaved plants is recommended to protect the eggs. Fry are initially very small and require infusoria or Paramecium as a first food.
